claim: Stanford RegLab/HAI study found legal hallucination rates of 69% (GPT-3.5) to 88% (Llama 2) on verifiable federal-court questions; ≥75% on case-holding queries.
quote: Legal hallucinations are alarmingly prevalent, occurring between 69% of the time with ChatGPT 3.5 and 88% of the time with Llama 2 when these models are asked specific, verifiable questions about random federal court cases.

claim: Purpose-built legal AI tools still hallucinate: Lexis+ AI >17%, Westlaw AI-Assisted Research >34%.
quote: Lexis+ AI produced incorrect information more than 17% of the time, and Westlaw AI-Assisted Research hallucinated more than 34%.

claim: Damien Charlotin's AI Hallucination Cases tracker logs 486+ court cases globally where filings relied on hallucinated citations; 324 in U.S. courts as of late 2025.
quote: 486 cases worldwide... 324 of them in U.S. federal, state and tribal courts.

claim: Mata v. Avianca (2023): Judge Castel fined attorneys Schwartz and LoDuca $5,000 after their ChatGPT-drafted brief cited fictitious cases with fabricated quotations.
quote: The Court dismissed a personal injury case against the airline Avianca and issued a $5,000 fine to the plaintiffs' lawyers who had submitted fake precedents generated by ChatGPT.

claim: California (Sept 2025): a state-court appeal brief had 21 of 23 case quotations fabricated; the attorney was fined $10,000 — the largest such California sanction.
quote: A California attorney must pay a $10,000 fine for filing a state court appeal full of fake quotations generated by ChatGPT... 21 of 23 quotes from cases cited in the attorney's opening brief were made up.

claim: AI-tainted court filings rose from a few per month in early 2025 to a few per day by late 2025.
quote: When tracking began earlier in 2025, a researcher encountered a few cases a month, but now sees a few cases a day.

claim: Moffatt v. Air Canada (BC Civil Resolution Tribunal, Feb 2024): Air Canada was held liable for negligent misrepresentation when its chatbot invented a bereavement-fare refund policy.
quote: On February 14, 2024, the British Columbia Civil Resolution Tribunal found Air Canada liable for misinformation given to a consumer through the use of artificial intelligence chatbots.

claim: Koenecke et al., 'Careless Whisper' (FAccT 2024): ~1% of Whisper transcribed segments contained hallucinations; ~40% of those were harmful (violent language, invented medications, racial slurs).
quote: 187 segments contained hallucinations... about 1% of test cases. Nearly 40% of the hallucinations are harmful or concerning.

claim: OpenAI's Whisper is used by ~30,000 clinicians and 40 health systems via Nabla, which deletes the original audio after transcription.
quote: Whisper is used by around 30,000 clinicians and 40 health systems to document interactions with patients.

claim: Mass General Brigham JAMA Network Open study (April 2026, 21 LLMs, 29 cases, 16,254 responses): all models failed differential diagnosis from initial symptoms >80% of the time.
quote: All of the models failed to produce an appropriate differential diagnosis more than 80% of the time.

claim: Mount Sinai (2025): when fed false medical premises, leading chatbots elaborated on the falsehoods rather than correcting them, fabricating supporting clinical detail.
quote: AI chatbots can run with medical misinformation, study finds, highlighting the need for stronger safeguards.

claim: Knight First Amendment Institute review of 78 election deepfakes: cheap fakes (slow-mo clips, recontextualised footage) were used 7× more than AI-generated content in 2024 election misinformation.
quote: Cheap fakes that didn't use AI were used seven times more often than AI-generated content.

claim: Slovakia 2023: a fake audio of Progressive Slovakia leader Michal Šimečka 'rigging' the election surfaced in the 48-hour pre-election silence period; he lost despite leading polls.
quote: Two days before the election, a fake audio clip surfaced... the clip went viral, its impact amplified by the timing just before the election during Slovakia's electoral 'silence period'.

claim: Aug 2024: X's Grok wrongly told millions of users that Kamala Harris had missed ballot deadlines in 9 states; the error persisted >1 week before correction.
quote: Grok wrongly told social media users that Vice President Kamala Harris had missed the ballot deadline in nine states... able to repeat the misinformation for more than a week.

claim: Arup (Feb 2024): a Hong Kong finance worker wired HK$200M (~US$25.6M) across 15 transfers after a deepfaked CFO and colleagues on a video call instructed him to.
quote: A finance worker, was duped into attending a video call with people he believed were the chief financial officer and other members of staff, but all of whom turned out to be deepfake re-creations.

claim: Jan 2024: explicit AI deepfakes of Taylor Swift were viewed 47M+ times on X before takedown; X temporarily blocked her name from search.
quote: One post reported to have been seen over 47 million times before its eventual removal.

claim: Vectara hallucination leaderboard (Apr 2026): on document-summarisation factuality, frontier models still hallucinate — Claude Opus 4.5 10.9%, GPT-4.1 5.6%, Gemini-2.5-flash-lite 3.3%.
quote: Claude-opus-4.5: 10.9%... GPT-4.1: 5.6%... Gemini-2.5-flash-lite: 3.3%.

claim: Brennan Center: AI's biggest near-term election risks are voter-targeted scams and impersonation rather than mass persuasion.
quote: Gauging the AI Threat to Free and Fair Elections.

claim: Harvard Ash Center post-2024 review: AI was used in 80%+ of countries that held elections in 2024, but the predicted disinformation 'apocalypse' did not materialise.
quote: More than 80 percent of countries experienced observable instances of AI usage relevant to their electoral processes.

claim: Stanford CIS analysis of the hallucination case database: 90% of offending firms are solo or small practices; 56% of cases are plaintiff-side; ~50% of cases with named tools used ChatGPT.
quote: The vast majority of law firms involved – 90% – are either solo practices or small firms. In 56% of the cases, the AI hallucinations were attributed to the plaintiff's counsel.
